title: Traditional rug hooking basics
subtitle: Pulling wool strips through monk's cloth with a rug hook
excerpt: Traditional rug hooking pulls narrow strips of wool fabric through a woven backing using a hooked tool. Each pull creates a loop on the surface; a field of loops builds up into a dense pile fabric. The technique is worked freehand or from a drawn design transferred to the backing.
sourceNotes: Traditional rug hooking with wool strips on hessian or monk's cloth is documented extensively in North American and British craft traditions from the 19th century onward. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A tool with a handle and a small hooked metal tip used to pull wool strips through a woven backing to form a pile. Rug hooks come in a range of hook sizes to suit different strip widths.
A stable, evenly woven cotton fabric with a basket-weave structure used as backing for rug hooking. Its regular weave provides consistent spacing for pulling wool strips through.

A narrow strip of wool fabric cut from recycled clothing or yardage for rug hooking. Standard widths range from 3 mm (fine, cut-6 gauge) to 8 mm (medium, cut-10 gauge) depending on the hook size and desired pile density.
The surface structure of a hooked rug, formed by a series of loops of wool strip pulled through the backing to a consistent height. The loops sit upright and create a dense, textured surface.

content.text: . The backing is stretched in a frame to keep it taut during hooking. Each strip is pulled through from below, leaving a small loop above the backing surface. Loops are typically 5-8 mm tall, pulled consistently to give an even surface.

items.name: Monk's cloth or hessian backing, cut 10 cm larger than the finished rug on all sides
Wool strips, 4-6 mm wide, cut from recycled 100% wool fabric or rug wool yardage
Sharp scissors (for cutting strips and trimming)
Marker pen or transfer paper (for the design)

content.text: Step 1: Prepare the backing and frame

Draw or transfer your design onto the monk's cloth using a permanent marker. Mount the backing in the rug frame, pulling it taut in all directions. A slack backing produces uneven loops and a puckered finished rug. Overlock or tape the raw edges of the backing before framing to prevent fraying during hooking.

Hold a wool strip under the backing in your non-dominant hand. Push the hook down through a hole in the backing from the top. Hook the strip and pull the tail end up through the backing to the surface (about 2 cm tail sticking up). Move to the next hole along (skip one hole for close-packed pile; skip two for slightly open pile). Push the hook through and pull a loop of the strip up to the surface about 5-7 mm high. Continue pulling loops along the strip.

When you reach the end of a strip, pull the tail end up through the backing (same as the starting tail). Tails and loops should all be the same height on the surface. Do not knot or secure the tails: the density of loops around each strip keeps everything locked in place. Trim tails to the same height as the loops.

Hook the outline of each colour area first, then fill the interior. Work in flowing curved rows that follow the design shape rather than straight grid lines: organic hooking lines give a more natural-looking finished surface. Maintain consistent loop height throughout.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two problems specific to rug hooking.

items.symptom: Loops pull back down to the underside when the hook is withdrawn
items.cause: The hook is being withdrawn at an angle, dragging the loop with it.
items.fix: Withdraw the hook straight upward, perpendicular to the backing surface. The loop should remain standing once the hook is clear.

items.symptom: Backing puckers or distorts as hooking progresses
items.cause: The backing was not stretched taut enough in the frame, or it was removed and re-framed without re-tensioning.
items.fix: Stop and re-tighten the backing in the frame before continuing. Steam the puckered area lightly with an iron to relax the distortion, then re-tension.
